takeaway: First Round defines growth hacking as data-driven experimentation optimized for measurable results at speed, coined by Sean Ellis in 2010 because startups needed "someone who could obsess over new users, test every channel at their disposal and drive measurable results fast" rather than a brand marketer.

FourWeekMBA frames growth as a cross-functional process: "Growth hacking is a process of rapid experimentation, coupled with the understanding of the whole funnel, where marketing, product, data analysis, and engineering work together to achieve rapid growth" — distinct from broad brand-awareness campaigns.

The Sean Ellis Score measures product-market fit via one survey question; the bar is "The goal is to reach a stage where at least 40% of your users would answer 'Very disappointed' if they could no longer use your product" — growth should wait until you clear it.

Sean Ellis (in Lenny's interview) insists product-market fit precedes scaling, then invests sequentially in activation, engagement, referral, revenue, and finally acquisition; you "dig into the users who would be 'very disappointed'... aiming to grow your 'very disappointed' user group to 40% of all your users."

The AARRR pirate funnel is "a simple and effective reference model to understand the five key stages of the customer lifecycle" — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — introduced by Dave McClure of 500 Startups in his 2007 "Startup Metrics for Pirates" talk.

Growth teams use the AARRR funnel to find the weakest stage: "A good funnel has the ability to measure every step in a highly objective way," so you instrument each stage, spot the biggest drop-off, and focus experiments there rather than spreading effort evenly.

Growth loops reframe growth as compounding rather than linear: "A Growth Loop is a closed, self-reinforcing system in which the outputs of one cycle of user activity become the inputs for the next cycle of acquisition or engagement" — popularized by Reforge's Brian Balfour and Andrew Chen.

A North Star Metric is "a single, critical measurement that reflects the long-term value a company provides to its customers," used to align every team on one number that captures customer value rather than a vanity metric like raw signups.

Microsoft Research ties controlled experiments to the North Star: experiments establish causality between a product change and the outcome — "Experimentation enables focus on what matters, while using scientific discipline to control what we can at intermediate steps."

Sean Ellis's ICE framework scores each experiment idea on Impact (effect on the North Star), Confidence (evidence it will work), and Ease (effort), then ranks by the average; "A low score in any category is ideally offset in at least one other category."

A/B testing is a randomized controlled experiment: "A/B testing is employed to compare multiple versions of a single variable... to determine which of the variants is more effective," using statistical hypothesis testing so a winner is real signal, not noise.

The LTV/CAC ratio compares the profit a customer generates over their life to the cost of acquiring them; the healthy benchmark is ~3.0x — "for every $1 spent on sales and marketing, the company generated $3 of additional gross contribution."

Customer acquisition cost (CAC) "measures the expense required to persuade a customer to buy a product or service," calculated by dividing total sales-and-marketing spend by the number of customers won in a period — the denominator of the unit-economics check.

Product-led growth uses the product itself as the acquisition engine: "Product-led growth (PLG) uses the product as the primary driver to acquire, activate, retain, and expand customers—often through freemium or free trials that let users experience value before paying," the model Wes Bush's book defined.

Brian Balfour argues retention is the foundation that multiplies every other lever: "If you have terrible retention, the other parts of the engine don't really matter" — leaky retention means acquisition spend pours into a bucket with a hole.

Customer retention is a company's ability to keep customers from leaving across the full lifecycle, and it pays: research cited holds that "engaged customers generate 1.7 times more revenue than normal customers," making retention a growth lever, not just a support concern.
